content:
Our agent hallucinated a refund.

We had built a support bot on LangChain to handle order issues.
It worked in staging.
It failed on day 3 in production.

One user asked for a partial refund on a $48 order.
The agent replied with confidence, approved $120, and wrote a fake policy citation.
Our logs showed 17 similar attempts over the next hour.

We first blamed the model.
Then we tried a bigger model, tighter prompts, and a second verification step.

None of it fixed the core issue.

The real problem was simpler:
the agent had write access before it had proof.

It could read the order system, reason about the case, and act.
But there was no hard boundary between suggestion and execution.
One bad tool call was enough.

We changed one thing:
the agent could draft a refund request, but Stripe approvals moved behind a human gate.

The next week, refund errors dropped to zero.

My lesson from that outage:
AI agents are not broken because they think poorly.
They break when we let them act with more confidence than our systems can justify.

Where do you draw the line between agent autonomy and human approval?

Simple architecture beats clever architecture.

I learned that the hard way on a backend rewrite.

We had 14 services, gRPC between teams, and a beautiful diagram in Miro.
It looked like a senior engineer had painted it.

Then one small change took 11 days to ship.

The bottleneck wasn’t code.
It was coordination, versioning, and everyone being afraid to touch the seams.

We cut it down to 3 services and moved two workflows back into a single Rails app.
Not because microservices are bad.
Because our team was too small for the shape we’d built.

Deploys dropped from 6 steps to 1.
On-call pages fell by 40% in the next month.

The contrarian part:
architecture is not a trophy for maturity.
It’s a cost you pay every day.

The strongest argument for complexity is scale.
Sometimes that’s true.

But most teams I’ve seen don’t need more boundaries.
They need fewer places to fail.

What architecture choice looked smart on paper and slowed you down in practice?

We shipped a broken migration.

It touched 18,000 customer records in one night.

The script looked fine in staging.
In production, one missing WHERE clause started rewriting the wrong rows.

Support lit up at 2:13 a.m.
Finance froze refunds.
Engineering spent 11 hours restoring backups from Postgres and re-running jobs by hand.

The cost was not just the incident.
We lost 3 days of roadmap time and a customer who had trusted us with their billing data.

The mistake wasn’t “moving too fast.”
It was shipping a change that could not prove it was safe before it touched real data.

That one still follows me.

Now, before any migration goes live, I want a dry run, a row count, and a rollback plan that a tired person can execute at 2 a.m.

The lesson: if a change can corrupt trust, the burden is on the code to prove it deserves prod.

What’s the worst production mistake that changed how you ship?

I stopped trusting vibe checks.

We were building an AI support bot, and the real problem was simple: how do we tell if it’s actually helping people, or just sounding confident?

That meant comparing two eval tools: LangSmith, the tracing and testing tool from LangChain, and OpenAI Evals, OpenAI’s benchmark runner.

LangSmith was faster to set up.
We had traces on the same day, and could see exactly where answers drifted across 200 customer tickets.

OpenAI Evals was stricter.
It took longer to wire in, but it forced us to write clean test cases and score the model the same way every run.

After 2 weeks, the winner was clear.

LangSmith helped us debug.
OpenAI Evals helped us decide.

When we needed one source of truth for whether a change was better or worse, OpenAI Evals won.
It was less pretty, but more honest.

The lesson: use the tool that makes bad news obvious.
Pretty dashboards are nice.
Repeatable scores keep you from fooling yourself.

Which eval tool has saved you from a false win?

We shipped the dashboard again.

It’s a React app for small ops teams who live in spreadsheets all day.

This week I rebuilt the slowest screen, the one that opens before the morning standup and decides whether people trust the product.

I cut the first load from 4.8 seconds to 1.9.

The change was boring in the best way.

I split one huge React component into smaller pieces, moved the heaviest chart behind a lazy load, and swapped a couple of “just render everything” habits for plain old loading states.

The weird part: the app looked almost the same.

But the behavior changed fast.

Support tickets about “the page froze” dropped from 11 last week to 3 this week.

What’s still broken?

Our filter bar is still doing too much work on every keystroke, and on older laptops you can feel it.

That’s the part I’m fixing next.

The lesson: users don’t care how elegant your React code feels.

They care about whether the screen appears before they give up.

What’s the slowest screen you’ve ever had to make feel fast?
